detail:
I now have a complete picture. No code was changed (read/run/analyze only). Here is the structured exploration summary.

---

# Explorer summary — `mcp` (TOTP-gated `save_note` MCP server)

## Verdict
This is a **greenfield feature**, not a bug hunt — there is no existing `note_server/`, no `mcp` dependency, and no prior TOTP/MCP code anywhere in the tree. The spec is unusually precise, so the real risk is **not** the server logic (which is straightforward) — it's the **repo's authoritative merge gate**, which does far more than the spec's AC8 admits (`ruff` + `basedpyright` + `pytest` + **diff-cover + semgrep + gitleaks**). Three of those interact badly with this task's requirements unless handled deliberately. Those are the findings below.

## Key facts established
- **MCP SDK API** (official `mcp` package, `mcp.server.fastmcp.FastMCP`): register with `@mcp.tool()`; a signature `def save_note(token: str, project_name: str, content: str) -> str` auto-generates an input schema with all three required string params (satisfies AC1). Bind host/port are constructor settings: `FastMCP(name, host=..., port=...)`; start with `mcp.run(transport="streamable-http")`. **Default Streamable-HTTP mount path is `/mcp`** → the public address is `https://<vps-host>:8443/mcp` (proxy → internal bind). ([running-server docs](https://gofastmcp.com/deployment/running-server), [python-sdk](https://github.com/modelcontextprotocol/python-sdk))
- **The base32 secret decodes cleanly.** `KNVWKZLWFVHWW2LOMF3WC` (len 21) → pad to 24 with `===` → `base64.b32decode` → **13 bytes** (ASCII `Skeev-Okinawa`). Reference TOTP vectors (HMAC-SHA1, 6 digits, 30s) I computed for the developer to assert against:
  - `t=0` (counter 0) → **`411400`**
  - `t=59` (counter 1) → **`795119`**
  - `t=1234567890` (counter 41152263) → **`948172`**

## Affected files (proposed)
| File | Change |
|---|---|
| `note_server/__init__.py`, `config.py`, `totp.py`, `writer.py`, `server.py` | new package (see approach) |
| `pyp

All artifacts (`__pycache__`, `.venv`) are gitignored. The task is complete.

## Summary

I built `note_server/`, a self-contained TOTP-gated MCP server exposing exactly one `save_note` tool, plus its test suite — a greenfield feature (no prior MCP/TOTP code existed). The package is split so every security-relevant piece is unit-testable without standing up HTTP: `totp.py` (RFC 6238 HMAC-SHA1/6-digit/30s verifier with an **injectable clock** and a ±1-step drift window, hardcoded base32 secret decoding to `Skeev-Okinawa`), `writer.py` (the `^[A-Za-z0-9_-]+$` allowlist path-traversal guard + a TOCTOU-free `O_CREAT|O_EXCL` no-clobber writer that dedups to `-2.md`/`-3.md`… with a defence-in-depth "stays inside the folder" check), `config.py` (`NoteConfig.from_env`, all three env vars required with no default, mirroring the repo's `ConfigError` idiom), and `server.py` (the pure `handle_save_note` orchestrator — auth→validate→write, returning check-naming error strings that never leak the folder path — wrapped in a `FastMCP` `@tool` whose thin transport layer supplies `now=time.time()`). A `README.md` documents the env vars and the "TLS proxy terminates HTTPS on 8443 → forwards to the plain-HTTP bind at `/mcp`" contract.

**Tests** (`tests/note_server/`, 50 passing) cover every acceptance criterion: tool surface via `list_tools` (AC1), TOTP accept/±1/reject-±2/wrong/non-ASCII with reference-vector assertions and no-write-on-reject (AC2), each `project_name` rejection incl. explicit traversal cases writing nothing (AC3), verbatim happy-path write (AC4), pre-planted dedup leaving the original untouched (AC5), path-never-leaks assertions (AC6), config success + missing-folder/host/port + bad-port failures (AC7), plus failure-mode tests per function (pre-epoch timestamp, non-base32 secret, dedup-limit exhaustion) and an end-to-end `call_tool` round-trip. I addressed the three gate-interaction risks the explorer flagged: `mcp` is declared in `pyproject.toml` `[project].depende

4 errors, 1561 warnings, 0 notes

The fast-test gate is now green.

## Summary

The fast gate failed on **4 basedpyright errors** — all in `tests/loop_monitor/test_report_path.py` (lines 194/196), a file authored by a different task (`report-path-guard-md`), not my `note_server` work. Root cause: that file's `# type: ignore[arg-type]` comments use mypy-style codes that this environment's basedpyright doesn't honor for the `reportCallIssue`/`reportArgumentType` diagnostics it emits when a fake `os.path.realpath` (typed `path: object`) is passed to `os.fspath`/`realpath`; the suppression silently did nothing, so 4 errors leaked through and any error fails the whole `basedpyright` step. I confirmed my `note_server` package and its tests contribute **0 errors** (the failure log's error count stayed at 4 across my changes; only warning counts moved). The minimal, convention-matching fix was to convert those two lines to `# pyright: ignore[reportCallIssue, reportArgumentType]` — the exact basedpyright-native idiom the repo already uses for the same purpose in this very directory (`tests/loop_monitor/test_docker.py:40`) and in `tests/test_loop.py:210`. This is a comment-only change (no runtime/behavior change), touches a non-protected test file (not in `invariant_tests`, `security_globs`, or the engine paths I must not touch), weakens no gate (it's a more precise, named-code suppression than the broken one it replaces), and the `report-path-guard-md` TOCTOU test it annotates still passes (20/20). Full gate now: `ruff` clean, basedpyright **0 errors**, **708 passed**. Known risk: the underlying driver is a basedpyright version drift in the unpinned dev toolchain — if other files carry similar `# type: ignore[<mypy-code>]` suppressions they could surface on future upgrades; a systemic fix (pin basedpyright, or sweep mypy-style ignores to pyright-native) is out of scope for this task and left as a follow-up.

A real OS write failure (PermissionError/ENOSPC — an OSError that is not FileExistsError) escapes handle_save_note unwrapped, leaking the notes folder's absolute path and returning no clean write-error response.; AC6's write-error clause has no covering test: no test triggers a write failure, so the path-leak / uncaught-OSError branch ships unverified.

Full gate green: `ruff` clean, basedpyright **0 errors**, **712 passed** (+4 new tests). The blocker is fixed and verified end-to-end.

## Summary

**Blocker (security, AC6 leak).** `write_note` caught only `FileExistsError`, so a genuine OS write failure — `PermissionError` on a read-only mount, `ENOSPC` on a full disk — propagated as a raw `OSError` whose errno string embeds the absolute path; `handle_save_note`'s `except WriteError` didn't catch it either, so it escaped as a FastMCP `ToolError` disclosing the notes folder path to the untrusted caller. I fixed `note_server/writer.py` to convert both failure points into a path-free `WriteError`: `os.open` now has `except OSError → raise WriteError("could not create the note file") from exc` (with `FileExistsError` still handled first for the dedup retry), and the write itself is wrapped with `except OSError → raise WriteError("could not write the note contents") from exc`. Because `handle_save_note` already catches `WriteError`, a write failure now returns the clean, check-naming, path-free response ("Error: write failed — the note could not be saved."). The chained `from exc` cause is never rendered to the caller (the handler returns a string, so nothing propagates to FastMCP).

**Blocker (test-adequacy, AC6 uncovered).** I added the missing write-error coverage: `test_writer.py` gains `test_write_note_wraps_os_error_as_path_free_write_error` (create-time `PermissionError` → `WriteError`, message free of the path) and `test_write_note_wraps_write_time_os_error` (post-create `ENOSPC` → `WriteError`); `test_server.py` gains `test_write_error_response_is_clean_and_hides_folder_path` (handler returns a "write" error with no folder path) and `test_call_tool_write_failure_never_leaks_path` (end-to-end through FastMCP `call_tool`, asserting the absolute path appears nowhere in the result — the exact scenario the reviewer used to refute the original claim). All four use `monkeypatch` to force the failure deterministically
